name: Col de Port and Col de Légrillou Loop
name_original: Entre le col de Port et de Legrillou -  Parc naturel des Pyrénées Ariégeoises
description: This difficult 50.4-mile (81.1 km) road cycling route in the Pyrenees Ariégeoises Natural Park offers challenging climbs and wild scenery.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Saurat are situated within the Ariège Pyrenees Regional Natural Park, offering access to challenging terrain and varied cycling experiences. The commune's territory ranges from 621 to 1941 meters above sea level, featuring demanding ascents and panoramic views of the southern Pyrenees chain. This area is characterized by its mountainous landscapes, including the Col de Port and the Calamès mountain, with the Saurat river flowing through its valleys. Best road cycling routes around Saurat The most popular road cycling route is Col de Port and Col de Légrillou Loop, a 50.4 miles (81.1 km) trail that takes 4 hours 39 minutes to complete. This route features significant elevation gain through Pyrenean landscapes. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Foix – Greenway of Montels loop from Brassac, a moderate 30.8 miles (49.5 km) path. This route offers varied terrain, including sections along a greenway. Local road cyclists also love the Notre-Dame de Sabart Chapel – Devil's Bridge loop from Les Cazals, a 34.7 miles (55.9 km) trail leading through areas with cultural landmarks, often completed in about 2 hours 30 minutes. Road cycling around Saurat is defined by challenging ascents like the Col de Port, mountainous Pyrenean landscapes, and picturesque villages. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in Saurat are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 200 reviews. More than 8,500 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Saurat's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How challenging are the road cycling routes around Saurat?
A: Road cycling in Saurat is known for its challenging terrain, with routes ranging from 621 to 1941 meters above sea level. You'll encounter demanding ascents like the famous Col de Port and the formidable 'Mur' (Wall) of Péguère, which features gradients up to 16%. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e939133384/col-de-port-and-col-de-legrillou-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Col de Port and Col de Légrillou Loop&lt;/a&gt; involves over 1900 meters of elevation gain.

Q: What are the different difficulty levels for road cycling routes in Saurat?
A: Saurat offers a variety of road cycling routes catering to different skill levels. You'll find approximately 16 easy routes, 43 moderate routes, and 39 difficult routes, ensuring options for beginners to experienced cyclists looking for a challenge.

Q: What natural features and landmarks can I expect to see along the road cycling routes?
A: While cycling around Saurat, you'll be immersed in the stunning Pyrenean landscapes within the Ariège Pyrenees Regional Natural Park. Routes often pass through picturesque villages and offer panoramic views of the southern Pyrenees chain from higher elevations. You might also encounter historical sites like the St. Mary Magdalene church in Saurat, known for its Romanesque influences. Notable mountain passes include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/418635" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Col de Port&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/122431" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Péguère Pass&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes around Saurat are designed as loops. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e939133384/col-de-port-and-col-de-legrillou-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Col de Port and Col de Légrillou Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a popular option, as is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/36306901" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Foix – Greenway of Montels loop from Brassac&lt;/a&gt;, offering varied terrain including greenway sections.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about their experience in Saurat?
A: The road cycling routes in Saurat are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 200 reviews. Cyclists often praise the challenging ascents, the breathtaking Pyrenean landscapes, and the variety of terrain available.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly road cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, despite the region's reputation for challenging climbs, there are approximately 16 easy road cycling routes around Saurat suitable for beginners or those looking for a more relaxed ride. These routes still allow you to enjoy the beautiful surroundings without extreme elevation gains.

Q: Can I find routes that pass by cultural landmarks?
A: Absolutely. Some routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/41158700" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Notre-Dame de Sabart Chapel – Devil's Bridge loop from Les Cazals&lt;/a&gt;, lead through areas rich in cultural landmarks. Saurat itself has the historic St. Mary Magdalene church, and the region has a past significant for its forges and mines, with traces still visible along ancient paths.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Saurat?
A: The Pyrenean region, including Saurat, is generally best for road cycling during the warmer months, typically from late spring to early autumn. Many ascents benefit from shaded areas, making them more enjoyable even during warmer periods. However, always check local weather conditions, especially for higher passes, as they can be affected by snow or adverse weather outside of peak season.

Q: Are there specific routes that offer exceptional panoramic views?
A: Yes, from higher elevations along many routes, you can revel in wonderful panoramic views of the southern Pyrenees chain. Routes that include significant climbs, such as those around the Col de Port or the Pic des Trois Seigneurs, are particularly known for their expansive vistas.

Q: Are there any specific mountain passes I should aim to cycle?
A: Saurat is situated within the Ariège Pyrenees Regional Natural Park, offering access to mythical passes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/418635" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Col de Port&lt;/a&gt; (1250 meters) is a notable climb, and the 'Mur' of Péguère is famous for its steep gradients. Other passes in the vicinity include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/211842" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Agnès Pass&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/211843" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lers Pass&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Saurat in total?
A: There are nearly 100 road cycling routes available around Saurat, offering a wide range of distances, elevations, and difficulty levels to explore the stunning Pyrenean landscapes.

Q: Can I find routes that are relatively shorter for a quicker ride?
A: Yes, if you're looking for a shorter ride, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/37676336" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roadbike loop from Arignac&lt;/a&gt; cover about 20 kilometers and can be completed in a couple of hours, offering a good option for a quicker cycling experience.
tips: This pass is one of the best known in the Ariège Pyrenees in general, and in the L'Arize massif in particular.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, the 'Between Port Pass and Legrillou Pass' route is classified as a "Hard road ride" and is primarily suited for cyclists with very good fitness. It involves significant elevation gain and challenging climbs, making it unsuitable for beginners.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the route?
faqs.answer: Cyclists can expect breathtaking and wild scenery. The route traverses the heart of the Ariège forest, offering tranquility and a refreshing atmosphere. You'll encounter rocky landscapes, diverse flora and fauna, and stunning panoramic views as you ascend the passes, particularly from the upper section of Col de Port.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the 'Between Port Pass and Legrillou Pass' cycling route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas for this exact route are not detailed, you would typically look for parking near the start or end points of the passes, such as around Col de Port or Col de Légrillou. Many villages in the Pyrenees Ariégeoises Natural Park offer public parking. For more detailed information on parking in the region, you might consult local tourism resources like tourisme-couserans-pyrenees.com.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best seasons to undertake this ride are spring and autumn. During these times, the weather is typically mild, and the natural landscapes display vibrant colors, enhancing the cycling experience.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for cycling in the Pyrenees Ariégeoises Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on public roads within the Parc Naturel Régional des Pyrénées Ariégeoises. However, always respect local regulations, especially concerning protected areas and private property. As a natural park, it's important to practice Leave No Trace principles.

faqs.question: Is the 'Between Port Pass and Legrillou Pass' route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route itself is a road cycling path, the Pyrenees Ariégeoises Natural Park generally welcomes dogs, often on a leash, in many areas. However, for a challenging racebike route like this, bringing a dog along on the bike might not be practical or safe. If you're traveling with a dog, consider shorter, less strenuous routes or designated walking trails within the park.

faqs.question: How challenging are the climbs on Col de Port and Col de Légrillou?
faqs.answer: The climbs are quite challenging. Col de Port, at 1250m, features an average gradient of 8% on its ascent, with sections rarely dropping below 7% and peaking around 10%. Col de Légrillou (938m) involves a 5.4 km climb with a positive elevation of 374 meters, an average gradient of 6.87%, and a maximum of 15%.

faqs.question: What is the road surface like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The road surfaces on this route are mostly well-paved and generally easy to ride. The Ariège Pyrenees are known for offering excellent cycling roads, though some segments on harder routes in the region might occasionally be unpaved. This specific route primarily uses paved roads suitable for racebikes.

faqs.question: Are there places to refuel or find refreshments along the route?
faqs.answer: While the route itself traverses wild and tranquil areas, the Pyrenees Ariégeoises region has villages and towns where you can find cafes, restaurants, and accommodation. It's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially for the longer, more remote sections, and plan your stops in advance.

faqs.question: Does this cycling route intersect with other official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with other official trails. For instance, you will encounter parts of the 'Tour du moulin', 'Le Picou depuis Brassac', and 'Tour de La Barguillère' trails along the way.

faqs.question: What makes this specific route notable compared to other cycling options in the Pyrenees Ariégeoises?
faqs.answer: This route is considered a "wild pass," being less known but highly enjoyable. It combines demanding climbs with serene natural beauty, offering a challenging yet rewarding experience away from more crowded areas. The region's drivers are also typically courteous and accustomed to sharing the road with cyclists.
